cards.concept: Key concepts from Chapter 25 - Virtual Functions
cards.explanation:
Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism in C++:
  • Base pointers/references can call derived functions
  • Use 'virtual' keyword in base class
  • Override in derived classes (use override specifier)
  • Resolved at runtime via vtables
  • Essential for polymorphic behavior

cards.text: The three key components of polymorphism in C++ are: {{c1::virtual functions}}, {{c2::inheritance}}, and {{c3::base class pointers/references}}.

cards.explanation:
• Always use override specifier in derived classes
  • Make destructors virtual in polymorphic base classes
  • Never call virtual functions from constructors/destructors
  • Use final sparingly to prevent further overriding
  • Pass polymorphic objects by pointer/reference, not value
  • Delete copy operations to prevent slicing

cards.front: What are the main performance costs of virtual functions?
cards.back:
• Runtime overhead for vtable lookup (2 indirections)
  • Memory overhead (vptr per object, vtable per class)
  • Cannot be inlined
  • Larger object sizes

cards.text: A class with at least one {{c1::pure virtual function}} is an {{c2::abstract base class}} and {{c3::cannot be instantiated}}.

cards.front: What are three common mistakes with virtual functions?
cards.back:
• Forgetting to make destructor virtual
  • Signature mismatch (wrong const, parameters, etc.)
  • Passing derived objects by value (object slicing)

cards.question: Will this code work correctly for polymorphism?
cards.explanation: Shape::area() is virtual, Circle overrides it correctly, destructor is virtual, and we're calling through a pointer - all requirements for polymorphism are met.

cards.concept: When to use different inheritance techniques
cards.explanation:
• Virtual functions: Different behavior for derived classes
  • Pure virtual: No sensible base implementation
  • Abstract base class: Define interface/contract
  • Interface class: Multiple inheritance of pure interfaces
  • Virtual base class: Solve diamond problem (rare)

cards.front: What is the fundamental problem that virtual functions solve?
cards.back: They allow base class pointers/references to call derived class functions, enabling runtime polymorphism. Without virtual functions, base pointers always call base class versions.

cards.text: Virtual functions use {{c1::late binding}} (resolved at runtime), while non-virtual functions use {{c2::early binding}} (resolved at compile time).

cards.concept: The relationship between virtual functions and the Liskov Substitution Principle
cards.explanation:
Virtual functions enable the Liskov Substitution Principle: derived classes can be used anywhere the base class is expected.

Requirements:
  • Derived classes maintain the base class contract
  • Overrides have same/compatible signatures
  • Behavior is substitutable (not just syntax)
This is the foundation of polymorphic design.
examples: std::cout << s.area();  // Works for Circle, Rectangle, etc.
// Any Shape derivative can be passed

cards.front: What is object slicing and how do you prevent it?
cards.back:
Object slicing: when a derived object is assigned to a base object by value, losing derived parts.

Prevention:
  • Pass by pointer or reference
  • Delete copy constructor/assignment in base
  • Use smart pointers for polymorphic objects

cards.question: Identify all errors in this code:
cards.answer: Errors: 1) func() should be virtual in Base 2) override fails because base isn't virtual 3) Pass by value causes slicing 4) No virtual destructor in Base
cards.explanation: Multiple issues prevent polymorphism: non-virtual function, object slicing from pass-by-value, and missing virtual destructor.

cards.concept: Summary: Making a polymorphic class hierarchy
cards.explanation:
Complete checklist for proper polymorphic design:
  1. Mark functions virtual in base class
  2. Use override in derived classes
  3. Add virtual destructor to base
  4. Delete copy operations if appropriate
  5. Use pure virtual for interface functions
  6. Pass objects by pointer/reference
  7. Store in containers as pointers
  8. Consider using smart pointers
